description: The belief that Christ's body and blood are truly, really, and substantially present in the Eucharist, not merely symbolically.
resolution: Affirmed consistently in patristic sources; later controversy over mode of presence led to transubstantiation (1215) vs. Reformed memorialist views.

description: The church governance model where a single bishop holds supreme authority in a local church, distinct from the college of presbyters.
resolution: Became the universal model by mid-2nd century; debated in Protestant Reformation.

description: The doctrine that bishops are the direct successors of the apostles in an unbroken chain of ordination conferring teaching authority.
resolution: Affirmed by Catholic, Orthodox traditions; rejected by most Protestant traditions.

description: The title affirming that Mary bore God incarnate, not merely a human Jesus later united with divinity (Nestorianism).
resolution: Defined at Council of Ephesus (431); rejected by Church of the East (Nestorians).

description: The identification of Christ with the divine Logos of Greek philosophy, pre-existent and active in creation and revelation.
resolution: Became standard in Greek patristic theology; refined through Nicene and Chalcedonian definitions.

description: The affirmation that the Son is of the same substance (ousia) as the Father, rejecting Arian subordinationism.
resolution: Affirmed at Nicaea (325); contested through Arian controversy until Constantinople (381).

description: The doctrine that the Eucharist is a true sacrifice, not merely a commemorative meal, related to the sacrifice of the Cross.
resolution: Affirmed in Didache and Clement; developed by Cyprian; formally defined by Council of Trent (1545).

description: The use of the term "altar" (thusiasterion) for the table of the Eucharist, implying sacrificial character of Christian worship.
resolution: Universal in patristic usage; Reformers replaced with "table" terminology.

description: The doctrine that baptism effects spiritual rebirth and the forgiveness of sins, not merely symbolizing them.
resolution: Affirmed by virtually all patristic writers; debated in Reformation over infant baptism and mode.

description: The doctrine that God is one Being in three co-equal, co-eternal Persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.
resolution: Terminology developed by Tertullian; dogmatically defined at Nicaea (325) and Constantinople (381).

description: The doctrine that all humans inherit a fallen nature from Adam, resulting in a tendency toward sin and separation from God.
resolution: Developed by Augustine against Pelagius; affirmed by Council of Carthage (418) and Council of Orange (529).

description: The determination of which books constitute authoritative Scripture for Christian faith and practice.
resolution: Athanasius' Festal Letter 367 lists NT canon; confirmed at Councils of Hippo (393) and Carthage (397).
